perturbation.original_text: d)All sales made by the Agent of the Kallo Mobile care suite of products shall create a Book of Business owned by the Agent, which constitutes all funds due to Agent under this agreement flowing from each sale for a period not to exceed 36 months from the date of acceptance by Kallo of the Client EULA or until the EULA terminates under the provision that are outlined therein of the Kallo Mobile care suite of products.
perturbation.changed_text: d) All sales made by the Agent of the Kallo Mobile care suite of products shall create a Book of Business owned by Kallo, which constitutes all funds due to Agent under this agreement flowing from each sale for a period not to exceed 36 months from the date of acceptance by Kallo of the Client EULA or until the EULA terminates under the provision that are outlined therein of the Kallo Mobile care suite of products.
perturbation.explanation: Switching ownership of the 'Book of Business' from the Agent to Kallo creates a conflict, as the term is specifically defined as belonging to the Agent. This affects commission structures and rights to future revenue.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts basic contract law principles regarding the interpretation of contracts. Specifically, under UCC § 2-202 (Parol Evidence Rule) and general contract law, the express terms of a contract, including defined terms, are given considerable weight. Changing the ownership of the 'Book of Business' directly contradicts its original definition within the agreement, creating ambiguity and potentially rendering the provision unenforceable.
perturbation.location: 2.KALLO SALES AGENT PROGRAM TERMS AND CONDITIONS
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts basic contract law principles. The original text defines the 'Book of Business' as owned by the Agent. The changed text reassigns ownership to Kallo, which is a direct contradiction of the original definition. The Law Explanation cites UCC § 2-202 (Parol Evidence Rule), emphasizing that express terms of a contract are given considerable weight. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this by stating that terms in a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement'. By changing the ownership, the modified contract violates this principle, because the 'Book of Business' is given a different meaning, which makes the change a legal contradiction, as it violates the original definition.

perturbation.original_text: Should the parties disagree as to what constitutes "sufficient marketing efforts", the issue will be submitted to binding arbitration. This course of action is not intended to be a means for Page 12 Kallo, its successors or assigns, to take over the successful operation of Agent's business generated under this agreement without fair and just compensation.
perturbation.changed_text: Should the parties disagree as to what constitutes "sufficient marketing efforts", the issue will be submitted to non-binding mediation. This course of action is intended to be a means for Page 12 Kallo, its successors or assigns, to take over the successful operation of Agent's business generated under this agreement without any compensation.
perturbation.explanation: Switching from 'binding arbitration' to 'non-binding mediation' fundamentally alters the dispute resolution process and reversing the compensation clause. The new statement implies Kallo has the right to take over the Agent's business without compensation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is present in every contract, including those governed by the UCC. Specifically, UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Stating that Kallo intends to take over the Agent's business without compensation demonstrates a lack of good faith and fair dealing, rendering the provision likely unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the obligation of good faith, violating UCC § 1-304. The original text ensures 'fair and just compensation,' while the altered text explicitly states 'without any compensation,' indicating an intention to exploit the Agent's business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement", confirms that the UCC requires good faith in all contracts. Therefore, the modified contract, by allowing Kallo to take over the Agent's business without compensation, violates this obligation. This creates a legal contradiction because the modification removes a core protection for the agent, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
